Solution to Question 3: cb ~ 2
e —e

The structure involved in causing futching gait, ifthe child is gluteus medius (marked as 2).
|

The gluteus medius originat & from the outer surface of the ilium between the anterior and
posterior gluteal lines “ra of the muscle i is at the lateral surface of the greater trochanter. It
is supplied by the Sup or glutegknerve.

Gluteus medius ey minimiig’ae powerful abductors of the hip joint. Mechanism of actions of
gluteus medius : and minigus:

¢ When the leg on one side is lifted off the ground, the gluteus medius and minimus muscles, on
the opposite supported side, exert traction on the hip bone by acting from the femur. This action
results in a slight raising of the pelvis on the unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk in
the upright position.

* The actions of these muscles are affected when there is an injury to the superior gluteal nerve,
neck of the femur, or loss of normal angulation of the shaft of the femur. This occurs in conditions
like paralysis of the glutei, developmental dysplasia of the hip, etc.

¢ When the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis sinks down on the unsupported
(normal) side, when the patient tries to stand on the affected limb. This leads to a positive
Trendelenburg's sign with a characteristic lurching gait - the patient lurches towards the affected
side with contralateral pelvis drop.

Gluteal region

Solution to Question 4:

1070 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1071 ========================================
In the given clinical scenario, the inferior gluteal nerve is likely to be injured.

The gluteus maximus is innervated by the inferior gluteal nerve. Injury to the nerve causes
atrophy of the muscle causing weakness in the extension of the thigh. The patients typically have a
gluteus maximus lurch.

The gluteus maximus may be tested by extension of the hip against resistance, in the supine or
prone position.

Gluteus maximus muscle:

Origin:

* Posterior aspect of the dorsal ilium, posterior to the posterior gluteal line
* Posterior superior iliac spine

* Posterior aspect of sacrum and coccyx

¢ Sacrotuberous ligament.

Insertion - It is inserted mainly in fascia lata at the iliotibial band and also into the gluteal
tuberosity on the posterior femoral surface.

Action:

1072 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1073 ========================================
¢ Major extensor of the hip joint
¢ Lateral rotation of the hip joint
* Active in powerful abduction of the thigh

Arterial Supply - Inferior and superior gluteal arteries and the first perforating branch of the
profunda femoris artery.

Nerve Supply- Inferior gluteal nerve (L5, S1 and S2).

Solution to Question 10:

The posterior femoral muscles (hamstring muscles) are innervated by the sciatic nerve.

* Semitendinosus, semimembranosus, and the long head of biceps femoris are innervated by tibial
division of the sciatic nerve (L5, Si, and S2)

* The short head of biceps femoris is innervated by the common peroneal division of the sciatic

Solution to Question 11:

The short head of the biceps femoris is not attached to the ischial tuberosity. It is attached to the
lateral lip of the linea aspera, lateral supracondylar ridge of the femur, and the lateral
intermuscular septum of the thigh.

1078 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1079 ========================================
The ischial tuberosity is broadly divided into upper and lower areas:

¢ The upper area is further subdivided into a superolateral part for the semimembranosus and
an inferomedial part for the long head of the biceps femoris and the semitendinosus.

* The lower area is subdivided by an irregular vertical ridge, into lateral and medial areas. The
larger lateral area is for the adductor magnus. The smaller medial area is covered by adipose
tissue, which usually contains the sciatic bursa of the gluteus maximus, which supports the body

in sitting.

Solution to Question 12> a

The clinical fas are mags of piriformis syndrome, which occurs due to the entrapment of
the sciati ake

It is wigs by sitting a long time or running, climbing stairs, or applying firm pressure
directly over the piriformis muscle. Early signs include pain, tingling, or numbness in the
buttocks. Later it may lead to severe pain extending down the length of the sciatic nerve.

Solution to Question 15: “
The weakness of the vastus medialis is responsible for the lateral displacement of the patella.

The lowest fibers of the vastus medialis muscle, which are attached medially to the upper half of
the patella are referred to as vastus medialis obliquus. These fibers of the vastus medialis prevent
the lateral displacement of the patella, during knee motion. The weakness of the vastus medialis
obliquus may lead to patellar instability and pain.

Solution to Question 16:

Medial rotation of the flexed knee is produced by the following muscles:
¢ Semimembranosus
* Popliteus

¢ Semitendinosus

1081 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1082 ========================================
* Assisted by
¢ Sartorius
* Gracilis

Knee joint is a complex synovial joint consisting of tibiofemoral and patellofemoral articulations.
Movements occurring at knee joint are flexion, extension, medial rotation, and lateral rotation.

* Flexion - biceps femoris, semitendinosus, and semimembranosus (hamstrings)
¢ Extension - quadriceps femoris, assisted by tensor fasciae latae

* Medial rotation of the flexed leg - popliteus, semimembranosus, and semitendinosus

h

* Lateral rotation of the flexed leg - biceps femoris &

i a
Note: Semimembranosus tendinopathy may develop as a result of the overusé*Of muscle usually
cyclists and marathon runners. Patients may complain of,iiisidious pain.in-the posteromedial part
of the knee radiating distally to the medial calf or proximally to the posteromedial thigh. It is a

cause of chronic knee pain that often goes undiagnosed as it is poorly undeystood.

Solution to Question 18: . hn Ge
rad
Peroneus brevis muscle belongs sortie lateral compartment of the leg.

Muscles of,the ‘anterior compartment of the leg produce dorsiflexion (extension) of the ankle.
They are: =
¢ Extensor digitorum longus

¢ Extensor hallucis longus

¢ Tibialis anterior

* Peroneus tertius (inverts and everts foot)

Muscles of the posterior compartment produce plantar flexion. They include superficial and deep
groups which are separated by the transverse intermuscular septum.

¢ The superficial flexor group consists of the gastrocnemius, plantaris, and soleus.

* The deep flexor group consists of popliteus, flexor digitorum longus, flexor hallucis longus, and
tibialis posterior.

Muscles of lateral compartment evert the ankle and include peroneus longus and peroneus brevis.

Solution to Question 19:

Peroneus longus muscle is not involved in the inversion of the foot. It causes eversion and plantar
flexion.

Inversion of the foot is a movement in which the medial border of the foot is elevated so that the
sole faces medially.

¢ Tibialis anterior - Causes dorsiflexion and inversion of the foot
¢ Tibialis posterior - Causes plantar flexion and inversion of the foot

¢ Extensor hallucis longus - Causes extension of the great toe, dorsiflexion of the foot, and
inversion of the foot (minimal action)

1083 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1084 ========================================
Eversion of the foot is a movement where the lateral border of the foot is elevated so that the sole
faces laterally. Peroneus longus, peroneus brevis, and peroneus tertius are the muscles involved.

These movements take place, mainly at the talocalcaneal and the talocalcaneonavicular joints, and
partly at the transverse tarsal joint.

Solution to Question 21: i 4 ratte

The gastrocnemius (two heads) ‘and soleugahuscles are collectively known as triceps surae.
They constitute a powerful muscular mass*whose main function is plantar flexion of the foot.

The main bulk of the calf is formed by gastrocnemius, plantaris, and soleus muscles. These 3
muscles form the superficial flexot group of the "posterior compartment of the lower leg.

Solution to Question’ 22: "; a"

ine

t “i, \
Dorsiflexiietof the foot cafes pain in the deep posterior compartment syndrome of the leg.

All tibial fractures should be monitored for signs of compartment syndrome. Severe pain is the
most important clinical feature. Regular clinical evaluation is important because swelling,
blistering, and skin necrosis is associated with late, severe compartment syndrome.

Compartment syndromes of the lower limb:

* Superficial and deep posterior compartment syndrome - There will be pain on passive
dorsiflexion of the foot and on the toe extension

¢ Anterior compartment syndrome - There will be pain on passive plantar flexion of the foot and
toe flexion

¢ Lateral compartment syndrome - There will be pain on passive foot inversion.

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

The femoral nerve is a branch of the lumbar plexus and arises from the posterior (dorsal)
divisions of the ventral rami of L2—-L4.

1094 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1095 ========================================
The sciatic nerve, posterior femoral cutaneous nerve, and nerve to obturator internus are a part of
sacral plexus.

Solution to Question 2:

The given scenario is suggestive of meralgia paresthetica, in which the lateral cutaneous nerve of
the thigh is affected. The root value of this nerve is L2 and L3.

It arises from the posterior divisions of the ventral rami of L2 and L3 and supplies the skin of the
anterior and lateral surfaces of the thigh upto the knee.

nerve of the thigh. * o ee

Solution to Question 3:

The sciatic nerve is a branch of the sacral plexus. It is the largest branch of the sacral plexus.

Solution to Question 5:

The Nerve to pectineus arises near and passes below the inguinal ligament and then runs behind

and posterior divisions.

Solution to Question 8:

1098 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1099 ========================================
Loss of sensation of the medial side of the leg denotes injury to the saphenous nerve. It is a
branch of the posterior division of the femoral nerve.

The saphenous nerve is the largest cutaneous branch of the femoral nerve.

It arises from the posterior division of the femoral nerve. In the femoral triangle, it lies lateral to
the femoral artery. It then enters the adductor canal where it crosses anterior to the femoral
artery to lie medial to it. It leaves the adductor canal by piercing the fascia lata between the
gracilis and sartorius and supplies the skin over the patella.

It then descends along the medial border of the tibia along with the long saphenous vein and
supplies the skin on the medial side of the knee leg and foot as far as up to the first

metatarsophalangeal joint.
a

Solution to Question 9:

The anterior division of the obturator nerve does not innervate the adductor magnus. It is
supplied by the posterior division of the obturator nerve.

The obturator nerve arises from the anterior division of the ventral rami of L2-L4. Near the
obturator foramen, it divides into anterior and posterior branches.

¢ Anterior division supplies pectineus, adductor longus, adductor brevis, and gracilis

* Posterior division supplies adductor magnus, obturator externus. It also supplies adductor brevis
if the muscle is not supplied by the anterior division

The image given below shows the course of the obturator nerve.

Solution to Question 107“,
The sciatic nerve does not innervate the muscles of the gluteal region.

It is the largest nerve of the body and is made up of L4 to S3 spinal nerves of the sacral plexus. It
leaves the pelvis through the greater sciatic foramen, inferior to the piriformis. It descends in the
posterior thigh and divides into the tibial and common peroneal nerves proximal to the knee joint.

The tibial component of the sciatic nerve supplies:

¢ Semimembranosus

* Semitendinosus

* Ischial part of adductor magnus

* Long head of biceps femoris

The common fibular component of the sciatic nerve supplies a short head of the biceps femoris.

The images given below shows the course of the Sciatic nerve.

Solution to Question 11:

The given clinical scenario is suggestive of left superior gluteal nerve injury.

The patient shows characteristic lurching gait/ Trendelenburg's gait (positive Trendelenburg's
sign), which is seen in paralysis of gluteus medius and gluteus minimus muscles. These muscles
are supplied by the superior gluteal nerve.

When the leg on one side is lifted off the ground, the gluteus medius and minimus muscles, on the
opposite supported side, exert traction on the hip bone by acting from the femur. This action
results in the slight raising of the pelvis on the unsupported side, which helps maintain the trunk
in the upright position.

When the gluteus medius and minimus are paralyzed, the pelvis sinks down on the unsupported
(normal) side, when the patient tries to stand on the affected limb.

1101 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1102 ========================================
In the given case, when the patient lifts up his right leg and tries to stand on the left leg, his right
pelvis tilts down. This shows that the left side abduction mechanism is paralyzed and the right
side is normal. Hence, the left superior gluteal nerve injury is present in this case.

The image given below shows the Trendelenberg test.

Solution to Question 12: ‘ wo

* \
Foot drop is caused by-damage to the.,common peroneal nerve.

The common peroneal nerve is component of the sciatic nerve. In the posterior thigh, it supplies
the short death the biceps-femoris. It curves around the neck of the fibula deep to peroneus
longus andsdivides into Superficial and deep peroneal nerves.

* The superficial peroneal nerve innervates peroneus longus and peroneus brevis which are the
evertors of the foot and to the skin over the dorsal area of the foot except for the first webspace
and lateral side of the little toe.

¢ The deep peroneal nerve innervates all muscles of the anterior compartment which are the
dorsiflexors of the foot, extensor digitorum brevis, first two dorsal interossei muscles, and skin
between great and second toes.

Hence, injury to the common peroneal nerve will lead to:
¢ Weakness of dorsiflexion of foot or foot drop

* Loss of eversion

* Loss of extension of toes

* Sensory loss

¢ Anterior and lateral side of the leg

1102 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1103 ========================================
* Dorsum of the foot including the medial side of the great toe

Th image given below shows the course of common peroneal nerve.

Course of Common Peronest nerve

Solution to Question 13: ¢- : :

=a hs, oe a

The nerve affected in this patient is the-common fibular nerve (common peroneal nerve).
tae | #2

It is derived from the dorsal branches of the LA" L5 and Si - S2 ventral rami.

The sciatic nerve divides 4 othe tibial nerve and the common fibular nerve.

The common fibular herve runs opligively along the lateral side of the popliteal fossa to the fibular
head. It lies betweert the biceps ferhoris and lateral head of the gastrocnemius. It then curves
laterally argund the neck ofthe fibula, where it is most commonly injured (e.g., due to a tight
plaster cast): lh

It gets divided into superficial and deep fibular nerves.

Injury to the common peroneal nerve will lead to:

¢ Weakness of dorsiflexion of foot or foot drop

* Loss of eversion

* Loss of extension of toes

* Sensory loss

¢ Anterior and lateral side of the leg

* Dorsum of the foot including the medial side of the great toe

Solution to Question 14:

Peroneus longus is innervated by the superficial peroneal nerve. The rest of the muscles

Solution to Question 15:

Morton’s neuroma may occur due to the entrapment of branches of the medial plantar nerve.

It is the most common form of nerve entrapment in the foot. It is due to the entrapment of the
3rd common digital nerve which is a branch of the medial plantar nerve.

The medial plantar nerve can also be compressed at the Knot of Henry. It is the point where the
tendon of flexor hallucis longus passes deep to the tendon of flexor digitorum longus to reach the
medial side of the sole of the foot.

The image given below shows the Morton's neuroma.

Solution to Question 18:

The deep circumflex iliac artery is a branch of the external iliac artery, not the femoral artery.

The femoral artery is the continuation of the external iliac artery. It starts behind the inguinal
ligament midway between the anterior superior iliac spine and pubic symphysis.

It descends in the thigh anteromedially in the femoral triangle and passes thro the adductor
canal. It exits the adductor canal by passing through addugior hiatus, to reach the popliteal fossa

and becomes the popliteal artery. ‘. cn

. Pies

Solution to Quéstion 20: =,
Question 20:
A thrombus in the short saphenous vein may get dislodged and enter the popliteal vein. The
embolus may further travel into the right heart and eventually may cause a pulmonary embolism.

The short saphenous vein is the continuation of the lateral side of the dorsal venous arch. It
passes behind the lateral malleolus and drains into the popliteal vein in the popliteal fossa. The
popliteal vein continues as the femoral vein proximal to the knee.

The image given below shows the venous drainage of the lower limb.

Solution to Question 9:

The hunter’s canal lies beneath the sartorius muscle. It is also referred to as the subsartorial canal
or adductor canal.

The hunter’s canal is a triangular intermuscular tunnel occupying the lower two-thirds of the
medial aspect of the thigh.

Solution to Question 16:
Peroneus longus muscle maintains the lateral longitudinal arch and not the medial arch.
The muscles, tendons, and aponeurosis that maintain the longitudinal arches of the foot are of

two types:
* Slings: These structures help to maintain the arch by suspending the arch from above.

1123 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1124 ========================================
* Tie beams (bowstrings): These are present between the anterior and posterior pillars and help
maintain the arch by preventing the separation of the pillars.

Solution to Question 18:

Upper band is attached to medial malleolus of the tibia and not fibula.
Inferior extensor retinaculum:
* It is a Y-shaped band lying anterior to the ankle joint.

* The stem of the Y is at the lateral end and is attached to the upper surface of the calcaneus.

1124 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1125 ========================================
* The limbs of the Y are at the medial end. The proximal limb attaches to the medial malleolus
and the distal limb blends with the plantar aponeurosis.

* Peroneus tertius

Solution to Question 19:
Tibialis posterior tendon is the most medial structure passing through the canals in the flexor
retinaculum of the foot.

The flexor retinaculum of the foot converts grooves on the tibia and calcaneus into canals for the
tendons. The structures passing through canals formed by flexor retinaculum are as follows: (from
medial to lateral)

¢ Tendons of tibialis posterior
¢ Flexor digitorum longus

* The posterior tibial vessels

* The tibial nerve

* The tendon of flexor hallucis longus

Solution to Question 1:

Detailed Explanations

The typical cervical vertebrae are characterized by a foramen in the transverse process called the

foramen transversarium.

This foramen transversarium transmits the vertebral artery and vein, along with a branch from
the cervicothoracic ganglion (vertebral nerve). Lateral parts of the transverse procegs¢s of the

cervical vertebrae also contain the anterior and posterior tubercles.

Solution to Question 2:

Ee cal il
The vertebral column consists.of 33 ones. Gener ly, the number of vertebrae found in each
segment are as follows: AO —

*7 cervical eo we
+ 12 thoracic a je

¢ 5 lumbar ah wee

*5 sacral ro a

* 4 coccygeal oe {*

However, rhis total is subiéed to frequent variability, and there have been reports of variation
between 32 and 35 vertebrae.

The total length of vertebral column is about 70 cm in males and about 60 cm in females on an
average. Fibrocartilaginous intervertebral discs separate neighboring vertebrae in the cervical,
thoracic and lumbar region (except the first 2 cervical vertebrae). The vertebral column supports
the trunk, protects the spinal cord and nerves, and provide attachments for muscles. It is also an
important site of hematopoiesis throughout life.

The image given below illustrates the adult vertebral column.

The thoracic curvature is a primaty curve and is déveloped during embryonic life itself.

Both the primary curves (thoracic and pelwvie) that appear during embryonic life are convex
dorsally. After birth, functional mle development es in the appearance of secondary
cervical and lumbar spinal curvahey=in the sagitta plane.

Solution to Question 5:

The craniovertebral joints do not include the wings of the sphenoid.

These connect the cranium to the vertebral column.

¢ They include the atlanto-occipital joints and atlanto-axial joints.

* The bones involved include the occipital condyles, the atlas, and the axis.

These joints are specialized to provide greater mobility than seen in the rest of the axial skeleton.
Together, they function as a universal joint that allows horizontal and vertical scanning
movements of the head.

Occipitocervical fusion and stabilization surgery is the treatment of choice for patients with
occipitocervical pathologies including trauma, rheumatoid arthritis, congenital malformations,
neoplasms, and degeneration. High degree of mobility and the sharp angle at which the occiput
meets the upper cervical spine, makes rigid surgical fixation challenging.

1135 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1136 ========================================
Solution to Question 6:

The movements at the atlanto-axial joint allow a person to look left and right.
The atlanto-axial joints include 3 synovial joints:

¢ Median atlanto-axial joint- The central pivot joint between the dens of the axis and a ring formed
by the anterior arch and transverse atlantal ligament

* Lateral atlanto-axial joints- Plain synovial joints between lateral masses of atlas and upper facets

of the body of axis o.1

Movements at the atlanto-axial joint almost exclusively is rotation around the-axis, which help
side-to-side rotation of the head. The normal range of atlapipraxial rotation: is about 40°.

Solution to Question 7:

The transverse atlantal ligament is a component of the cruciform ligaments at the atlanto-axial
joints.
Cruciform ligaments are shaped like a cross:

¢ The horizontal component of the cross is formed by the transverse atlantal ligament. It is
attached on each side to the lateral mass of atlas. It retains the dens in position.

* The vertical component of the cross is formed by the superior and inferior longitudinal
ligaments.

¢ Superior longitudinal ligament arises from the upper margin of the transverse atlantal ligament
and inserts into the basilar part of the occipital bone.

¢ Inferior longitudinal ligament arises from the lower margin of the transverse atlantal ligament
and inserts to the posterior surface of the axis vertebra.

Solution to Question ey =

The given clinicahSebnario is uggéstive of a whiplash injury. In this, the anterior longitudinal
ligament oftheispine is damag d.

Whiplash is a form of cervical acceleration-deceleration injury. There is hyperextension followed
by flexion in the recoil. The main pathological features seen in these cases include tear

(strain) of the anterior longitudinal ligament and the capsular fibers of the facet joints in the
spine. In some cases, the intervertebral discs may be damaged. Fracture is seen in severe cases
only.

Anterior longitudinal ligament:
* It is a strong band that extends along the anterior surface of the vertebral bodies
¢ It extends from the basilar part of the occipital bone to the upper sacrum

¢ It is attached to the anterior tubercle of C1 and the body of C2

1137 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1138 ========================================
Membrana tectoria or tectorial membrane is the cranial continuation of the posterior longitudinal
ligament.

The posterior longitudinal ligament is present in the vertebral canal on the posterior surfaces of
the vertebral bodies. The posterior longitudinal ligaments stretch from the body of the axis (C2) to
the sacrum.

Above the level of axis, the posterior longitudinal ligament continues as membrana tectoria.
Membrana tectoria extends from the axis to the foramen magnum.

Solution to Question ge Ss

Rotation is anagoniially restrigtéd in the lumbar region.

The givedfinical vignet Eabupsestive of carcinoma prostate, which has metastasised to the
lumbar spine. Anatomically, the lumbar region of the vertebral column has a restricted range for
rotational movements.

Movements of the vertebral column:

* Flexion of the spine is accompanied by relaxation of the anterior longitudinal ligament and
compression of the anterior parts of the intervertebral discs. Flexion is effectively absent in the
thoracic region. Flexion movements are greater than extension or lateral flexion in the lumbar
region.

¢ Extension is marked in cervical and lumbar regions. It is significantly less at thoracic levels as
their discs are thinner and because of the ribs and chest musculature.

* Lateral flexion is flexion combined with coupled axial rotation. Lateral movements occur in all
parts of the column but are the greatest in the cervical and lumbar regions.

¢ Axial rotation is the highest in the cervical region, 50% of which occurs at the atlantoaxial joint.
All the other spine segments below the cervical have only a slight range of rotation, among which
the thoracolumbar junction has the most. The remainder of the lumbar region has very little
rotation.

The given clinical scenario is suggestive of Brown-Sequard syndrome caused due to injury to the
vertebral column by a bullet wound. The hemiazygous vein crosses from left to right at the level of
the T8 vertebra (and not T5).

Solution to Question 13:
he

“ah
The serratus anterior is not a boundary of the triangle of auscultation. As there are fewer
impedances between the examiner's stethoscope and the lungs, this area allows for better
auscultation of the patient.

Boundaries of the triangle of auscultation:

* Medially- trapezius muscle’s lateral border

* Laterally- scapula’s medial border

* Inferiorly- lattssisimus dorsi’s upper border

* Floor- 5th and 7th rib, 6th intercostal space, and the rhomboid major

The triangle overlies the inferior lobe of both lungs. For exposure of the floor of the triangle, the
patient needs to fold their arm across their chest to rotate the scapula laterally and bend forward
resembling somewhat a fetal position.

Solution to Question 3:

The clavicle is a modified long bone, as it has a modified shaft and no medullary cavity.
Long bones are classified as follows:

* Typical long bones (see figure) have an elongated shaft (diaphysis) and two expanded ends
(epiphysis). The shaft has a central medullary cavity and a nutrient foramen directed away from
the growing end. e.g., humerus, ulna, radius, femur, tibia, and fibula.

1154 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1155 ========================================
¢ Miniature long bones contain only one epiphysis. e.g., metacarpals, metatarsals, and phalanges.
* Modified long bones have a modified shaft or ends, and no medullary cavity. e.g., clavicle.
The following image shows a graphical illustration of a typical long bone:

Solution to Question 5:

The maxilla is a pneumatic bone.

Pneumatic bones contain air-filled cavities, which make them light-weight. They also aid in the

resonance of sound and act as air conditioning chambers for inspired air. e.g., maxilla, sphenoid,
ethmoid, etc.

Short bones: Carpal bones and tarsal bones
Flat bones: Sternum, ribs, scapula, and bones of the skull vault

Irregular bones: Hip bones, vertebra, and bones in the base of the skull.

1156 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1157 ========================================
Solution to Question 6:

Fabella is a sesamoid bone. This term is used to describe a bone embedded within a tendon or a
muscle. It is a variant of normal anatomy present in 10% to 30% of individuals. Fabella is found
within the tendon of the lateral head of gastrocnemius, where it moves over the lateral femoral
condyle.

Important sesamoid bones:

* Patella - largest sesamoid bone in the tendon of quadriceps femoris.
¢ Pisiform - tendon of flexor carpi ulnaris.

¢ Fabella - lateral head of the gastrocnemius. :

* Two sesamoid bones can be found in the first = within the tendons.of flexor pollicis
brevis and adductor pollicis. e, _

* Two sesamoid bones can be found in the first meatal is ulation within the
double tendon of flexor hallucis brevis. co oe
Fy

The X-ray image below depicts the fabella (m ior with an apn.

Solution to Question 7:

The Haversian system is seen in compact bones, not cancellous bones.

Based on their macroscopic structure, bones can be classified as compact and cancellous (spongy)
bones.

Compact bones are composed of cylindrical structural units called Haversian systems or

osteons. Each Haversian system contains a central canal within which the small blood vessels,
nerves, and endosteum are present. This canal is surrounded by multiple layers or lamellae of the
calcified matrix containing type I collagen fibers. These canals communicate with each other by
Volkmann's canal.

1157 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1158 ========================================
The lamellae of the cancellous/spongy bone are arranged in a meshwork, and hence do not form

Haversian systems.

The diaphysis and outer cortex of long bones are predominantly made of compact bone, while the
epiphysis and inner medulla are predominantly made of cancellous bone.

@]MARROW

The given X-ray shows Codman's triangle, which is due to an irregular periosteal reaction
(outermost layer), commonly seen in patients with osteosarcoma. The periosteum is a thick
fibrous membrane that covers the outer surface of all bones, except at the joint surfaces and
insertion points of tendons and ligaments (entheses). At the articular margin, the periosteum is

continuous with the capsule of the joint.

The periosteum has 2 layers:

1158 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1159 ========================================
* The outer fibrous layer - contains fibroblasts.

¢ The inner cambium layer (osteogenic layer) - contains progenitor cells that develop into
osteoblasts.

Periosteum gets nourishment from periosteal arteries. It has a rich nerve supply, which makes it
the most sensitive part of the bone.

Solution to Question 9:

Sharpey’'s fibers are thick collagen fibers that connect the periosteum to the underlying bone.
Sharpey's fibers are present in the bone and the teeth.
* Bone - tether the periosteum to the underlying bone.

* Teeth - connect the cementum to the periosteum of the alveolar bone (tooth socket).

Solution to Question 10:

Frontal bones undergo intramembranous ossification. Other bones listed have enchondral
ossification.

Based on their development, bones can be classified into 3 categories:

* Membranous bones (dermal bones) are formed through intramembranous ossification. There is
direct bone formation within highly vascular sheets or ‘membranes’ derived from mesenchymal
condensations. e.g., bones of the facial skeleton including the frontal, lacrimal, zygomatic, and
nasal bones. a

4
+ Cartilaginous bones are formed by intra-cartilaginous (enchondral) ossifigat@e Bone forms by
ossification of preformed hyaline cartilage model. This is isthe-main method-of bone formation in
the body. e.g., bones of limbs, vertebral column, and: thoracic cage.

* Membranocartilaginous bones ossify from both, éattilage and afleSenchymalicondensation.
e.g., Clavicle, mandible, occipital bone, tempotal bone, sphenboid bone, ete

Traction epiphysis is nonartiaghif ‘and does noe transmit weight. They provide attachment to one

Solution to Question 12: *
The structure marked X in the radiograph is the epiphyseal plate. It is the site for enchondral
ossification that is mainly responsible for the growth of long bones.

The epiphyseal plate (growth plate) is a hyaline cartilage layer that separates the epiphysis from
the metaphysis. It receives nourishment from both the epiphyseal and metaphyseal
arteries. Fractures involving epiphyseal plates are known as Salter-Harris fractures.

The growing ends of bones include:
* Upper limb: Upper end of humerus, and lower ends of radius and ulna

* Lower limb: Lower end of femur, and upper end of tibia

Solution to Question 13:

1160 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1161 ========================================
The fibula violates the law of ossification which states that the secondary ossification center that
appears first is the last to fuse.

The secondary ossification center at the lower end of the fibula appears before the ossification
center of the upper end and fuses before the upper end.

Ossification centers are of two types:

* Primary centers appear before birth, usually during late embryonic or early fetal life (seventh
week to the fourth month). They form the diaphysis.

* Secondary centers usually appear after birth. They form the epiphysis.

Solution to Question 16: om
Articular cartilage ee. being a type of hyaline cartilage lacks perichondrium.

Cartilage is a connective tissue composed of cells (chondrocytes) and fibers (collagen or yellow
elastic fibers). These are embedded in a firm, gel-like matrix that is rich in mucopolysaccharides.
It is much more elastic than bone. Cartilage does not have blood vessels or lymphatics.
Chondrocytes get their nutrition by diffusion. Cartilage has no nerves and is insensitive.

There are three types of cartilages:
¢ Hyaline cartilage

+ Elastic cartilage

¢ Fibrocartilage

Hyaline cartilage (except the subtype of articular cartilage) and elastic cartilage have
perichondrium. Fibrocartilage does not have perichondrium.

1162 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1163 ========================================
Solution to Question 17:

The septal nasal cartilage has hyaline cartilage and not yellow (elastic) cartilage.

Elastic cartilage (or yellow cartilage) contains a matrix rich in fine yellow elastic fibers containing
the protein elastin. It is present in:

Solution to Question 23: Le wae

A joint with three or WOR articular stittaces is classified as a compound joint.
Classification of joints based on the number of articulating surfaces:

* Simple joints have 2 arti ation surfaces. e.g., shoulder joint, hip joint.

* Compound joints have 3 or more articulation surfaces. e.g., elbow joint, wrist joint.

* Complex joints have an intra-articular disc between the articular surfaces which divides the joint
space. e.g., temporomandibular joint, sternoclavicular joint.

Solution to Question 24:

Gomphosis (dentoalveolar syndesmosis) is a peg-and-socket joint between a tooth and its socket.

Fibrous joints are joints where bones are joined together by fibrous tissue. They are further
classified as sutures, syndesmosis, and gomphosis (see Table).

Solution to Question 25:

The incudostapedial joint is a ball and socket type of joint.

In ball and socket type of joints, the articular surfaces include a globular head (male surface)
fitting into a cup-shaped socket (female surface). These joints are multi-axial and movements
occur around a number of axes. Flexion, extension, abduction, adduction, medial rotation, lateral
rotation, and circumduction, all occur quite freely. E.g., shoulder joint, hip joint,

1166 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1167 ========================================
talocalcaneonavicular joint, and incudostapedial joint.

Note: The other joint between the incus and malleus (incudomalleolar joint) is a saddle joint.

Solution to Question 26:

The deformity shown is hallux valgus. This is caused due to an excessive lateral deviation at the
first metatarsophalangeal joint. It is an example of an ellipsoid joint.

Ellipsoid joints have an oval, convex (male) surface fitting into an elliptical concave (female)
surface. They show biaxial movements such as flexion-extension, and abduction—adduction. A
combination of these movements results in cireumduction. Movement on the hind

prevented due to the general articular shape.

axis is

E.g., Wrist (radiocarpal) joint, atlanto-occipital joints, snciaeaxpophalaageal joints, and

metatarsophalangeal joints. 7, %, _ a",

Types of synovial joints ea

Solution to Question 27:

The talocalcaneonavicular joint is a ball and socket joint.
* The ball part is formed by the rounded head of the talus.
* The socket is formed by 3 structures:

* Posterior surface of the navicular

¢ Anterior articular surface of the calcaneus

* Upper surface of the plantar calcaneonavicular ligament (spring ligament).

Solution to Question 29:

The styloid process is a sharp elevation of bone.
Bony elevations can be sharp or rounded:

* Rounded or irregular elevations include a tubercle, tuberosity, epicondyle, malleolus,
and trochanter.

¢ Sharp elevations of bones include styloid process, spine, cornu (horn), and hamulus (hook-like).

Detailed Explanations

Solution to Question 1:

The origin of the muscle is fixed, while the insertion moves during contraction.

Skeletal muscles exert their force on the bones through their tendon attachments and the force
generated helps in generating movements or in maintaining posture.

Solution to Question 2:

1175 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1176 ========================================
Skeletal muscles are multinucleated, while smooth muscles are uninucleated.
There are three major muscle types in the human body:

¢ Skeletal muscle

* Smooth muscle

* Cardiac muscle

Solution to Question 4:

The rectus abdominis muscle contains parallel fasciculi. The other muscles listed have oblique
fasciculi.

Muscles can be classified based on the arrangement of muscle fibers relative to the direction of
pull as follows:

* Parallel - muscle fasciculi are parallel to the line of pull.

1176 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1177 ========================================
* Oblique - muscle fasciculi are oblique to the line of pull.

* Spiral - muscle fasciculi have a spiral or twisted arrangement.

Parallel muscles can be further grouped based on their shape as follows:

* Quadrilateral - parallel fibers run for a short distance.

¢ Strap-like - parallel fibers run for a long distance.

¢ Strap-like with tendinous intersections - parallel fibers are interrupted by tendinous insertions.

¢ Fusiform - parallel fibers in the muscle belly that converge at the tendon region.

Solution to Que tion's: 3°

The trapezitis muscle in ually is a flat, triangular muscle that extends over the back of the
neck and upper thorax. When paired together, they resemble a trapezium and hence the name.

1177 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1178 ========================================
Muscles with oblique fibers are further subclassified as follows:
¢ Triangular - They are triangular in shape.

* Pennate - They are feather-shaped. Based on their complexity, they can have the following
subtypes:

* Unipennate - fascicles on one side of the tendon.

* Bipennate - fascicles on two sides of the tendon.

¢ Multipennate - central tendon branches into two or more branches within the muscle.
* Circumpennate (radial) - muscle fibers converge to a central tendon in a radial fashion.

Solution to Question 6: ~~

The muscle marked X in the cadaveric image is the pectoralis major, which is a spiral muscle.
Spiral muscles have fiber architecture as follows:

¢ The muscle fibers have a spiral or twisted arrangement. e.g., pectoralis major (sternocostal
fibers) and latissimus dorsi that undergo a 180° twist between their medial and lateral
attachments.

* The entire muscle takes a spiral course around a bone. e.g., supinator winds obliquely around
the proximal radial shaft.

Cruciate is a type of spiral muscle where two or more planes of muscle fibers are arranged in
differing directions. e.g., sternocleidomastoid, masseter and adductor magnus are all partially
spiral and cruciate.

Solution to Question 11:

Autonomic efferents supply the smooth muscle fibres of the blood vessels.

Every skeletal muscle is supplied by a motor nerve, which actually is a mixed nerve that contains
motor and sensory fibres.

The motor supply of muscle contains the following efferents:
¢ Extrafusal muscle fibers are supplied by a-efferents that are large and myelinated.

* Intrafusal muscle fibers of muscle spindle are supplied by y-efferents that are small
and myelinated.

* Vascular smooth muscle fibers get autonomic efferents that are fine and unmyelinated.

Solution to Question 14:

Flexor carpi ulnaris is not a hybrid muscle as it is supplied by a single nerve (ulnar nerve).

A muscle supplied by two different nerves with different root values is called a composite or
hybrid muscle. These muscles usually have more than one set of fibers and get their supply from
different nerves for the different sets of fibers.

Examples of composite or hybrid muscles include:
* Brachialis
* Pectoralis major

* Iliopsoas

Solution to Question 16:

Muscle contraction without a change in muscle length is known as isometric contraction. The lady
is performing a plank exercise which is an isometric exercise to strengthen the abdominal
muscles.

Muscles can generate force through isotonic contraction or isometric contraction:

* Isotonic contraction involves maintaining constant tension as the muscle changes length. It can
be concentric or eccentric.

* Concentric contraction involves shortening of the muscle. Example: biceps and brachialis
contracting while lifting a weight by elbow flexion.

ne |
¢ Eccentric contraction involves lengthening of the muscle. Example: biceps and brachialis
lengthening while lowering the weight by elbow extension. at

* Isometric contraction involves force generation in the nucle while maintaining constant length.
Example: Holding a crouched position or doing a wall-sit exercise. C

Solution to Question 1:

Fenestrated capillaries are absent in the lungs.

1192 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1193 ========================================
Fenestrated capillaries have gaps between endothelial cells. They are present in certain tissues
that allow the passage of larger molecules such as the renal glomeruli, endocrine glands, intestinal
mucosa, and pancreas.

Solution to Question ey a

4
Muscular arterjgteS tontribute, torthe major part of peripheral resistance to blood flow.

The smallest arteries of t Sthuscular type are called arterioles. They have a tunica media that is
relatively thicker than the tunica adventitia. Arterioles cause the largest drop in blood pressure
before the blood flows into the tissue capillary beds. They are densely innervated by sympathetic
fibres. The contraction of arterioles is mainly regulated by local vasoactive and metabolic factors,
but central mechanisms also play a role.

tal }

Solution to Question 7:

The testicular veins have valves.

Valves within the veins maintain a unidirectional flow of blood, even against gravity. Congenital
absence of valves in testicular veins may be a predisposing factor for the development of
varicocele. The inferior vena cava also has a valve (the Eustachian valve) to direct the flow of
oxygenated blood towards the foramen ovale to escape the pulmonary circulation. After birth,
with the establishment of the pulmonary circulation and the increase in systemic vascular
resistance driving the closure of foramen ovale, the valve has no function and tends to regress.

The valves are absent in the following veins:

* Veins less than 2 mm in diameter

¢ Venae cavae

* Hepatic, renal, uterine, ovarian, cerebral, spinal, pulmonary, and umbilical veins.

The following image is a histological section showing the valve within a vein:

Solution to Question 9:

Compared to their corresponding arteries, the veins have a much lesser muscular and elastic
content. This explains the venous pressures being lower than the arterial pressures.

Solution to Question 10:

The thoracic and right lymphatic ducts are vessels of the lymphatic system which is referred to as

the drainage system of coarse type.

The lymphatic system helps remove larger particles such as proteins and particulate matter from

the tissue fluid. Therefore, they are considered as the drainage system of coarse type.

The venous system is considered as the drainage system of fine type.

Solution to Question 11:

The lymph nodes have both afferent and efferent lymphatics.

1198 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1199 ========================================
* The afferent lymphatics enter the lymph node at different parts of its periphery.
* The efferent lymphatics exit at the hilum.

Solution to Question 14:

1200 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1201 ========================================
There are 8 pairs of spinal nerves in the cervical region.

The cerebrospinal nervous system includes 12 pairs of cranial nerves and 31 pairs of spinal nerves.
The 31 pairs of spinal nerves are distributed as follows:

* Cervical nerves - 8 pairs

* Thoracic nerves-12 pairs

¢ Lumbar nerves-5 pairs

* Sacral nerves-5 pairs

* Coccygeal nerves-1 pair

Solution to Question 16:

The reflex arc consists of an effector, usually a muscle and not an affector.

A reflex arc is the basic functional unit of the nervous system which can perform an integrated
neural activity.

1201 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1202 ========================================
A mono-synaptic reflex arc is made up of:
* Receptor, e.g., skin

* Sensory or afferent neuron

¢ Motor or efferent neuron

¢ Effector, e.g., muscle.

Solution to Question 17: i “

\

Microglial cells do nobiriginate front the ectoderm but are derived from the mesoderm. Microglia
are possibly derived from the cifeilating monocytes which migrate into the CNS during the late
fetal and earl-postnatal lifes,

Neuroglial cells found in the parenchyma of the brain and spinal cord are broadly classified as
follows:

* Macroglia cells of ectodermal origin (neuroectoderm) including the astrocytes, oligodendrocytes,
and glioblasts.

* Microglia cells of mesodermal origin.

Solution to Question 18:

Capillaries with fenestrations are not part of the blood-brain barrier.
The blood-brain barrier is formed by:

* Capillary endothelium with tight junctions (no fenestrations).

* Basement membrane of the endothelium.

¢ End feet of astrocytes covering the capillary walls.

Solution to Question 20:

Dry skin is not expected to see due to sympathetic stimulation.

The sympathetic nervous system (SNS) arises from the lateral horn of Ti to L2 segments of the
spinal cord (thoracolumbar outflow). Sympathetic stimulation causes the following effects:

* Sweating due to secretomotor supply to sweat glands - Sudomotor
¢ Erection of hair due to contraction the arrector pili muscle - Pilomotor

* Raised blood pressure due to vasoconstriction - Vasomotor

1203 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1204 ========================================
* Dilated pupils
¢ Dry mouth
* Tachycardia

Common examples of sympathomimetic drugs include phenylephrine, albuterol, cocaine and
amphetamines.

Solution to Question 3:

The number of melanocytes does not affect the colour of human skin.

1211 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1212 ========================================
The racial differences in skin colour is determined by:

* Melanocyte morphology and activity.

* Number and size of melanosomes in melanocytes.

¢ The number of melanosomes individually dispersed within keratinocytes.

Melanin pigmentation is influenced by constitutive (genetically determined) and facultative
factors (reversible changes due to environmental factors like UV-rays, chemicals, and hormones).

Solution to Question 5:

The Merkel cells act as touch receptors. They are present in the basal layer of the epidermis and
outer root sheath of some big hair follicles and their functions are as follows:

* Act as slowly adapting mechanoreceptors in the skin that convey touch sensation.

* Detects the directional deformation of the epidermis and hair movement.

¢ Sense sustained pressure and edges of applied objects on the skin.

Solution to Question 8:

1214 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1215 ========================================
Apocrine sweat glands produce secretions that undergo bacterial decomposition to generate
odour.

Sweat glands are classified as follows:

¢ Eccrine sweat glands - The majority of sweat glands in the body are eccrine and open via
rounded pores to the skin surface.

« Apocrine sweat glands - They are present in the axillae, perianal area, areolae, periumbilical
skin, and genitalia.

Solution to Question 10:

The non-fibrous element of the extracellular matrix in the connective tissue is formed by the
ground substance.

Connective tissue consists of cells and extracellular matrix:

* The cells include fibroblasts, macrophages, plasma cells, mast cells, fat cells, and pigment cells.

¢ The extracellular matrix contains fibrous and non-fibrous elements.

1215 Sold by @itachibot

======================================== PAGE 1216 ========================================
* The fibrous element has three types of fibres - collagen, elastin and reticulin

* The non-fibrous element is formed by the ground substance

Solution to Question 11:

The statement ‘ligaments are devoid of nerve supply’ is false.

Ligaments have a rich nerve supply. This helps them function as sense organs and participate
in reflex mechanisms important for monitoring the position and movements of the joint. Intrinsic
ligaments surround the joint, whereas extrinsic ligaments are away and independent of the joint.

The majority of ligaments are made up of collagen fibres predominantly. These’are inelastic and
not stretchable. Ligamentum flavum and ligaments of auditory ossicles aresexceptions, as
they predominantly have elastin fibres that make them,elastic and strefefiable.

Solution to Question 13:
The linear fibrous band formed by interdigitation of the tendinous or aponeurotic ends of the
muscles is known as raphe.
Unlike most ligaments, the raphe are stretchable. Examples of raphe include:
* linea alba
* pterygomandibular raphe
¢ mylohyoid raphe
* median pharyngeal raphe
* anococcygeal raphe oe
ygeal rap AS
